context:
Page 28, Section 4 (Conclusions, Connections, and Comparisons)

To conclude, we stress that the analysis we did in this paper is only about bounding the number of edge steps and non-degenerate pivots.
Question 2. Is there a polynomial bound to the number of degenerate pivots performed by any of the pivot rules we presented when applied to 0/1-LPs?
title: Polynomial bound on degenerate simplex pivots for 0/1 polytope linear programs
background_latex:
Consider a linear program over a fixed feasible region that is a 0/1 polytope:
\[
\max\{c^\top x : x \in P\},\qquad P=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: Ax=b,\; Dx\le d\},
\]
where all extreme points (vertices) of \(P\) have coordinates in \(\{0,1\}\), and the input data \(A,b,D,d,c\) are integral.

To run the Simplex method, one may convert the LP to standard equality form by introducing slack variables (without requiring those slack variables to be 0/1 at vertices):
\[
\max\{(c')^\top x' : A' x' = b',\; x'\ge 0\},
\]
with \(A'\in\mathbb{Z}^{m'\times n'}\), \(b'\in\mathbb{Z}^{m'}\), \(c'\in\mathbb{Z}^{n'}\). A feasible basis \(B\subseteq [n']\) of size \(m'\) determines a basic feasible solution (BFS) \(x'\). A simplex pivot exchanges one entering nonbasic variable with one leaving basic variable, producing a new basis (and a new BFS).

A pivot is called non-degenerate if it changes the current vertex (equivalently, the step length in the pivot direction is positive), and degenerate if the basis changes but the BFS remains at the same vertex (step length zero). Degeneracy can cause cycling or long sequences of degenerate pivots (stalling).

The paper introduces specific pivot rules for 0/1-LPs (variants of steepest-edge and shadow rules) and proves polynomial bounds on the number of non-degenerate pivots; however, the number of degenerate pivots (basis exchanges that do not move to a different vertex) may still be large and is not bounded in their analysis.
problem_definition_latex:
For at least one of the pivot rules introduced for 0/1-LPs (e.g., the paper's modified steepest-edge or modified shadow pivot rules), determine whether there exists a polynomial upper bound (in the input size, or at least in \(n,m'\)) on the total number of degenerate pivots (degenerate basis exchanges) that the Simplex method can perform when solving
\[
\max\{c^\top x : x \in P\},\qquad P\text{ a 0/1 polytope as above}.
\]
Equivalently, bound by a polynomial the number of iterations in which the basis changes but the current BFS (and hence the current vertex of \(P\)) does not change, when the Simplex method is run with one of these pivot rules on arbitrary 0/1-LPs.

open_problem_quote:
To conclude, we stress that the analysis we did in this paper is only about bounding the number of edge steps and non-degenerate pivots.
Question 2. Is there a polynomial bound to the number of degenerate pivots performed by any of the pivot rules we presented when applied to 0/1-LPs?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Given a concrete pivot rule, one can computationally generate families of 0/1-LPs and simulate the pivot sequence to search for instances with many degenerate pivots (stalling). Detecting degeneracy and counting degenerate basis exchanges is straightforward during simulation, enabling large-scale automated searches for superpolynomial growth patterns.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate counterexample families (constraint matrices/objectives) can be programmatically parameterized, and a fitness signal can be the measured number of degenerate pivots under the specified pivot rule. An evolutionary loop can mutate instances to maximize stalling while preserving the 0/1-vertex property (or approximating it with verifiable checks on small sizes).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Black–De Loera–Kafer–Sanità (2021) introduce pivot rules tailored to 0/1 polytopes—True Steepest-Edge and two Shadow variants (Slim/Ordered)—and prove strongly polynomial bounds on the number of non-degenerate pivots: steepest-edge paths are strongly polynomial (via circuit-augmentation results), Slim Shadow uses at most \(n\) non-degenerate pivots, and Ordered Shadow uses at most the dimension \(d\). Their analysis explicitly avoids perturbations because 0/1 polytopes are typically highly degenerate, and they isolate degeneracy as the remaining obstacle: a simplex implementation may still perform many degenerate basis exchanges while staying at the same vertex of the original polytope.

The most directly relevant forward-citing progress is the work “On the number of degenerate simplex pivots” and its journal version “On the number of degenerate simplex pivots: K. Kukharenko and L. Sanità”. These papers do not analyze the specific 0/1-polytope pivot rules of Black–De Loera–Kafer–Sanità, nor do they bound the total number of degenerate pivots over an entire run. Instead, they design an anti-stalling pivot rule for general standard-form LPs that guarantees at any non-optimal BFS the number of consecutive degenerate pivots is at most \(n-m-1\) (or \(\min\{n-m-1,m-1\}\) under stronger directional information). This shows that degeneracy can be controlled locally (stalling cannot persist too long) even without 0/1 structure, but it leaves open whether the original True Steepest-Edge / Slim Shadow / Ordered Shadow pivot rules admit polynomial bounds on total degenerate pivots on arbitrary 0/1-LPs.

Related work on smoothed analysis (e.g., “Optimal smoothed analysis of the simplex method”) largely sidesteps degeneracy by random perturbations that make degenerate pivots occur with probability 0, and work on monotone path polytopes (e.g., “Monotone Paths on Polytopes: Combinatorics and Optimization”) clarifies the combinatorics of coherent (typically nondegenerate) shadow paths. Neither yields worst-case polynomial bounds on degenerate basis exchanges in the deterministic 0/1 setting. Consequently, the specific open question posed as Question 2 in the 0/1-polytope simplex paper remains open: proving a polynomial upper bound on the total number of degenerate pivots for their pivot rules, or constructing superpolynomial lower bounds, remains a key gap.

sources.title: On the number of degenerate simplex pivots
sources.theorem_or_result: They prove existence of an “antistalling” simplex pivot rule for any LP in standard form \(\min\{c^\top x:Ax=b,\ x\ge 0\}\) that bounds the number of consecutive degenerate simplex pivots at any non-optimal BFS by \(n-m-1\) (and in a strengthened version by \(\min\{n-m-1,\ m-1\}\)), where \(n\) is the number of variables and \(m\) the number of equality constraints (Theorems 1–2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial upper bound on the total number of degenerate pivots when solving 0/1-polytope LPs under specific pivot rules (modified steepest-edge/shadow rules). This paper does not analyze those specific 0/1-polytope pivot rules, nor does it bound the total number of degenerate pivots over the whole run. Instead it gives a general technique/pivot rule that guarantees a polynomial bound on stalling: at any non-optimal vertex, the number of consecutive degenerate pivots before a non-degenerate move is at most \(O(n)\). This is directly relevant as it shows degeneracy can be controlled (in the sense of consecutive degenerate pivots) even for general LPs, hence also for 0/1 polytopes. However, it does not by itself yield a polynomial bound on the total number of degenerate pivots for the pivot rules in the original 0/1-polytope paper; total degenerate pivots could still be large if many vertices are visited, and their rule requires access to an improving feasible direction (potentially as hard as solving the LP).

sources.title: On the number of degenerate simplex pivots: K. Kukharenko and L. Sanità
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves that for any LP in standard equality form \(\min\{c^\top x: Ax=b,\ x\ge 0\}\) with \(A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}\), there exists an (anti-stalling) simplex pivot rule such that at any non-optimal extreme point the number of consecutive degenerate simplex pivots is at most \(n-m-1\) (Theorem 1), and in fact at most \(\min\{n-m-1,\ m-1\}\) when an improving feasible direction of the form \(\tilde x-x\) between vertices is available (Theorem 2). It also bounds the number of degenerate pivots needed at an optimal vertex to reach an optimal basis by \(n-m\) (Lemma 4).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial upper bound on the total number of degenerate pivots when solving LPs over 0/1 polytopes using one of the specific pivot rules from Black–De Loera–Kafer–Sanit\`a (modified steepest-edge/shadow rules). This paper does not analyze those specific 0/1-polytope pivot rules. Instead, it introduces a different pivot rule (Algorithm 1) that, given access to an improving feasible direction at the current vertex, guarantees a polynomial bound on degeneracy: it prevents long stalling by bounding the length of any run of degenerate pivots by \(O(n)\), independent of integrality/0-1 structure. Thus it provides strong partial progress toward the spirit of the open problem (controlling degeneracy), but it does not resolve whether the original 0/1-specific pivot rules themselves admit polynomial bounds on degenerate pivots, nor does it yield a polynomial bound on the total number of degenerate pivots without additional assumptions (e.g., availability of improving feasible directions, and bounds on the number of visited vertices).

sources.title: Optimal smoothed analysis of the simplex method
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves improved smoothed-analysis bounds for a (semi-random) shadow-vertex simplex method: there exists a simplex method with smoothed complexity (number of pivot steps) at most \(O(\sigma^{-1/2} d^{11/4} \log(n)^{7/4})\) (up to additional \(d^3\log(n)^2\) terms), and it gives a nearly matching lower bound \(\Omega(\sigma^{-1/2} d^{1/2} \ln(4/\sigma)^{-1/4})\) on the (semi-random) shadow size / diameter after smoothing for instances with \(n=\lfloor(4/\sigma)^d\rfloor\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a worst-case polynomial upper bound on the number of degenerate simplex pivots when solving integral 0/1-polytope LPs under specific pivot rules (modified steepest-edge or modified shadow rules) after converting to standard form with slack variables. This citing paper studies a different setting: smoothed analysis of a shadow-vertex-type simplex method for general real LPs under Gaussian perturbations, and it assumes (with probability 1) non-degeneracy of the shadow path due to random perturbations/objectives, so degenerate pivots essentially do not occur in its model. It does not analyze degeneracy in the worst case, does not specialize to 0/1 polytopes, and does not bound the number of degenerate basis exchanges for the pivot rules from the 0/1-polytope paper. At best, it provides techniques suggesting that random perturbations remove degeneracy and yield polynomial expected pivot counts, but this is not a polynomial bound on degenerate pivots in the original deterministic 0/1 setting.

sources.title: Monotone Paths on Polytopes: Combinatorics and Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives a combinatorial description of the vertices of the monotone path polytope \(M_c(\Delta(n,2))\) (for any generic direction \(c\)), yielding an explicit formula for the number of coherent monotone paths on the hypersimplex \(\Delta(n,2)\), and refines this by counting coherent monotone paths by their length \(\ell\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial upper bound on the number of degenerate simplex pivots under certain pivot rules for LPs over 0/1 polytopes. This paper studies monotone path polytopes, which encode the combinatorics of the shadow-vertex (shadow) pivot rule: vertices of \(M_c(P)\) correspond to coherent monotone paths that can occur as shadow-vertex pivot paths on \(P\) for some secondary direction \(\omega\). The results here are about enumerating coherent monotone paths on a specific 0/1 polytope family (hypersimplices), not about degeneracy or the number of degenerate basis exchanges in simplex on a standard-form formulation. Thus it provides structural/background information about possible (nondegenerate) shadow-vertex paths, but does not bound (or even model) degenerate pivots/stalling sequences.
